# ADR-001: Strategy Pattern for Node Rendering

<!-- Generated by seed-golden-context | Last updated: 2026-05-11 -->

## Status

Accepted

## Context

Rich Text fields in Contentful produce a recursive tree of typed nodes (Document, Paragraph, Heading1–6, Blockquote, Table, embedded entries/assets, etc.). The library needs to render this tree to an HTML string. The key challenge is extensibility: consumers will need to customize the HTML output for specific node types (e.g., rendering an `EmbeddedEntryBlock` as a custom component, or adding a CSS class to headings), without forking the library.

A single `switch` or `if/else` chain in a monolithic renderer would require consumers to subclass the renderer or copy-paste the whole rendering logic to customize a single node type.

## Decision

Use the **Strategy pattern** with a priority-ordered stack of `NodeRendererInterface` implementations. The main `Renderer` iterates the stack and delegates to the first `NodeRenderer` that returns `true` from `supports($node)`. Custom renderers are added via:
- `pushNodeRenderer()` — prepends to the stack (highest priority, overrides defaults)
- `appendNodeRenderer()` — appends to the stack (lowest priority, used for catch-all fallbacks)

Default renderers for all built-in node types are registered in `Renderer::createNodeRenderers()`. The same pattern is applied to parsing via `NodeMapper` instances registered in `Parser::createMappers()`, with `setNodeMapper()` for custom overrides.

## Consequences

- Consumers can override any node's rendering without subclassing: just push a custom `NodeRendererInterface` before the default.
- Custom renderers can inject any dependency (URL generators, template engines, etc.) in their constructor — no service locator needed.
- The `CatchAll` renderer pattern enables graceful degradation for unknown future node types — but it must be appended, not pushed, or it intercepts everything.
- Each renderer is responsible only for node types it explicitly `supports()` — concerns are cleanly separated.
- Adding a new node type requires adding a `Node`, `NodeMapper`, and `NodeRenderer` class — boilerplate is high but predictable and testable.
